subUnits.title: 5.1 Diffusion, Osmosis and Biological Importance
notes: Diffusion is the net movement of molecules or ions from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration, down a concentration gradient, until equilibrium is reached.
• Molecules move randomly due to kinetic energy
• Movement continues until concentration is equal throughout
• Occurs in gases, liquids, and across membranes
• Greater difference in concentration increases rate
• More space for molecules to pass through
• Gaseous exchange in lungs (O₂ and CO₂)
• Exchange of materials between cells and environment
• Distribution of substances within plant tissues
• Oxygen diffusion into blood in lungs
• Carbon dioxide diffusion out of blood
• Perfume sprayed in classroom spreads by diffusion
• Cooking aroma spreads through air by diffusion
• Oxygen diffuses into fish gills from water in Nigerian rivers

Osmosis is the net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration (dilute solution) to a region of lower water concentration (concentrated solution) through a selectively permeable membrane.
• Water molecules move across a membrane
• Movement is from dilute to concentrated solution
• Continues until equilibrium or pressure prevents further movement
• Cell shrinks (plasmolysis in plants; crenation in animals)
• Cell swells (turgid in plants; may burst in animals)
• Movement of water between plant cells
• Contractile vacuole function in Amoeba and Paramecium
• Root hairs absorb water from soil by osmosis
• Wilting occurs when plants lose turgidity during dry season
• Salted fish preserves food by osmosis (water leaves microbial cells)
Plasmolysis is the shrinkage of the cytoplasm away from the cell wall due to loss of water by osmosis when a plant cell is placed in a hypertonic solution.
• Plant cell placed in concentrated solution
• Water moves out of cell by osmosis
• Cytoplasm pulls away from cell wall
• If returned to water or dilute solution quickly
• Cassava or yam slices in salt solution become limp
• Wilting of crops during drought in Northern Nigeria
Haemolysis is the bursting of red blood cells when placed in a hypotonic solution due to excessive water intake by osmosis.
• Red blood cell placed in dilute solution
• No cell wall to prevent bursting
• Intravenous fluids must be isotonic to prevent haemolysis in Nigerian hospitals
• Malaria parasite causes haemolysis of red blood cells
Turgidity is the state of a plant cell when it is fully distended with water, with the cytoplasm pressing against the cell wall.
• Plant cell placed in hypotonic solution or water
• Keeps leaves spread out for photosynthesis
• Fresh vegetables are crisp and firm (turgid)
• Wilting occurs when cells lose turgidity during harmattan
Flaccidity is the state of a plant cell when it has lost water and is no longer turgid, but not yet plasmolysed.
• Cytoplasm no longer presses firmly against wall
• Vegetables left in sun become flaccid in Nigerian markets
Biological Importance of Diffusion and Osmosis:
• Root absorption of water and minerals
• Oxygen and carbon dioxide exchange in lungs
• Essential for plant growth and structure
• Teacher provides perfume and requests a student to spray at one end of the classroom
• Students at other end describe what happened
• Teacher demonstrates diffusion and osmosis using living and non-living components
• Use potato strips or pawpaw strips in concentrated salt/sugar solution
• Observe changes in length and firmness
• Place in water and observe recovery
• Note cytoplasm pulling away from wall
• 'Diffusion requires energy': Simple diffusion is passive; only active transport requires energy
• 'Osmosis is diffusion of solutes': Osmosis is specifically the diffusion of water
• 'All transport is the same': Passive and active transport have different mechanisms
• 'Plant cells can burst like animal cells': Plant cells have cell walls that prevent bursting
• Understanding osmosis helps manage irrigation and soil salinity in Nigerian agriculture
• Knowledge of diffusion explains drug absorption and action in Nigerian healthcare
• Osmosis principles used in food preservation (salting, sugaring) in Nigerian communities
• Water balance important for crop production in Nigerian climate
• Diffusion and osmosis are essential processes for cellular function
• These processes maintain water balance and facilitate material exchange
• Understanding these concepts is crucial for agriculture, medicine, and daily life
• In Nigeria, this knowledge supports crop management, food preservation, and health

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Diffusion osmosis cell environment biology SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Diffusion is the movement of molecules from:
quiz.explanation: Diffusion is the net movement of molecules from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration, down a concentration gradient.

quiz.q: Osmosis specifically involves the movement of:
quiz.explanation: Osmosis is the diffusion of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane from high to low water concentration.

quiz.q: When a plant cell is placed in a hypertonic solution, it undergoes:
quiz.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the plant cell by osmosis, causing the cytoplasm to shrink away from the cell wall (plasmolysis).

quiz.q: A red blood cell placed in a hypotonic solution will:
quiz.explanation: Red blood cells lack cell walls, so in a hypotonic solution, excessive water intake causes them to swell and burst (haemolysis).

quiz.q: Turgidity in plant cells is important because it:
quiz.explanation: Turgidity provides structural support for herbaceous plants, keeping them upright and leaves spread for photosynthesis.

quiz.q: A factor that increases the rate of diffusion is:
quiz.explanation: A steeper concentration gradient (greater difference in concentration) increases the rate of diffusion.

quiz.q: In Nigerian food preservation, salting fish works by:
opts: Osmosis removing water from microbial cells
quiz.explanation: Salting creates a hypertonic environment that causes water to leave microbial cells by osmosis, preventing their growth.

quiz.q: Root hairs absorb water from soil primarily by:
quiz.explanation: Root hairs absorb water from soil by osmosis, as the cell sap is more concentrated than the soil solution.

quiz.q: An isotonic solution is one where:
opts: Water concentration is equal inside and outside
quiz.explanation: In an isotonic solution, the solute concentration (and thus water concentration) is equal inside and outside the cell, so there is no net movement of water.

quiz.q: Understanding osmosis is important for Nigerian agriculture because it:
opts: Guides irrigation management and understanding of plant water relations
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of osmosis helps farmers manage irrigation, soil salinity, and understand crop water uptake and wilting.
